title: The Golden Rule Across Faiths
content: The principle of treating others as you wish to be treated is found in virtually every religious tradition. In Christianity: 'So in everything, do to others what you would have them do to you.' In Islam: 'None of you has faith until he loves for his brother what he loves for himself.' In Judaism: 'What is hateful to you, do not do to your fellow.' In Hinduism: 'This is the sum of duty: do not do to others what would cause pain if done to you.'

title: The Concept of Divine Love in Sufism
content: Sufism teaches that divine love (Ishq) is the essence of all spiritual paths. The 13th-century poet Rumi wrote: 'The religion of love is apart from all religions. The lovers of God have no religion but God alone.' This love transcends doctrinal boundaries and speaks directly to the heart of every seeker, regardless of their faith tradition.

title: Compassion as a Universal Virtue
content: Compassion is a cornerstone of every major faith tradition. Buddhism's Metta Sutta speaks of unlimited goodwill toward all beings. The Quran begins with 'In the name of God, the Most Compassionate, the Most Merciful.' The Jewish concept of 'rachamim' emphasizes mercy as a divine attribute. Christian teachings center on agape love - unconditional compassion for all.
source: Interfaith Peace Bridge - Comparative Theology Series

title: The Unity of Being: Wahdat al-Wujud
content: The Sufi doctrine of Wahdat al-Wujud, articulated by Ibn Arabi, teaches that all existence is a manifestation of the Divine. This perspective encourages seeing the sacred in all things and recognizing that apparent differences in religious forms veil a deeper unity. As the Quran says: 'Wherever you turn, there is the face of God.'

title: Peacemaking in Sacred Texts
content: Across religious scriptures, peacemaking is elevated as a sacred duty. The Bible declares 'Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called children of God.' The Quran states 'And if they incline to peace, then incline to it.' The Bhagavad Gita teaches that peace comes from within: 'Peace is not the absence of conflict, but the presence of justice.'
source: Interfaith Peace Bridge - Sacred Texts Collection
